Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For immediate release Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in October suggests that economic activity has been expanding at a moderate pace.
  · Household spending and business fixed investment have been increasing at solid rates in recent months, and the housing sector has improved further; however, net exports have been soft.
  · A range of recent labor market indicators, including ongoing job gains and declining unemployment, shows further improvement and confirms that underutilization of labor resources has diminished appreciably since early this year.
  · Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
  · Market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low; some survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations have edged down.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will continue to expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.
  · Overall, taking into account domestic and international developments, the Committee sees the risks to the outlook for both economic activity and the labor market as balanced.
  · Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
  · The Committee continues to monitor inflation developments closely.
  · The Committee judges that there has been considerable improvement in labor market conditions this year, and it is reasonably confident that inflation will rise, over the medium term, to its 2 percent objective.
  · Given the economic outlook, and recognizing the time it takes for policy actions to affect future economic outcomes, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative after this increase, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
  · This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
  · Yellen, Chair; William C.
  · Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; Charles L.
  · Evans; Stanley Fischer; Jeffrey M.
  · Lacker; Dennis P.
  · Lockhart; Jerome H.
  · Powell; Daniel K.
  · Tarullo; and John C.
  · Williams.

unchanged: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For immediate release Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in October suggests that economic activity has been expanding at a moderate pace.
Household spending and business fixed investment have been increasing at solid rates in recent months, and the housing sector has improved further; however, net exports have been soft.

A range of recent labor market indicators, including ongoing job gains and declining unemployment, shows further improvement and confirms that underutilization of labor resources has diminished appreciably since early this year.
Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
Market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low; some survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations have edged down.
Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.

The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will continue to expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.
Overall, taking into account domestic and international developments, the Committee sees the risks to the outlook for both economic activity and the labor market as balanced.
Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
The Committee continues to monitor inflation developments closely.

The Committee judges that there has been considerable improvement in labor market conditions this year, and it is reasonably confident that inflation will rise, over the medium term, to its 2 percent objective.

Given the economic outlook, and recognizing the time it takes for policy actions to affect future economic outcomes, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative after this increase, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
Yellen, Chair; William C.
Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; Charles L.
Evans; Stanley Fischer; Jeffrey M.
Lacker; Dennis P.
Lockhart; Jerome H.
Powell; Daniel K.
Tarullo; and John C.
Williams.

raw_response: {"report": "Headline CPI y/y has surged from near-zero to 1.24% over the last three prints, with the last two m/m readings (-0.11, -0.05) puzzling against that acceleration \u2014 the y/y jump is being driven by base effects (weak prints rolling off from a year ago) rather than fresh momentum. Indeed headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, essentially flat-to-falling on a recent-months basis, and headline_mom_3m_avg is -0.01%, both showing outgoing momentum is weaker than what's pushing the y/y rate up. This tells me the y/y acceleration is mostly a base-effect artifact of comparing against last year's energy-driven declines, and my prior call (if it anticipated continued acceleration) was directionally right on the print itself but the underlying momentum has already stalled. Core PCE remains the more telling read on persistence: it's basically flat at 1.19-1.20%, still 0.81pp below the 2% target, with no meaningful move in three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp). Core PCE shows no acceleration at all. Given headline's recent monthly prints are negative/flat and base effects from a year ago are now largely in the y/y number already, the room for further mechanical y/y acceleration is narrowing unless energy turns up again. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down on headline next print, with low-moderate conviction given base-effect uncertainty.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "headline_3m_annualized", "headline_mom_3m_avg", "core_pce_yoy", "core_yoy_change_3m", "core_distance_from_target"], "falsifier": "A rebound in energy prices or a strong positive headline m/m print (>0.2%) would signal renewed upward momentum, not just base effects.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "labor_tightness", "why": "Tighter labor markets could signal building wage-driven inflation pressure ahead of core PCE turning up."}], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: Headline CPI y/y has surged from near-zero to 1.24% over the last three prints, with the last two m/m readings (-0.11, -0.05) puzzling against that acceleration — the y/y jump is being driven by base effects (weak prints rolling off from a year ago) rather than fresh momentum. Indeed headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, essentially flat-to-falling on a recent-months basis, and headline_mom_3m_avg is -0.01%, both showing outgoing momentum is weaker than what's pushing the y/y rate up. This tells me the y/y acceleration is mostly a base-effect artifact of comparing against last year's energy-driven declines, and my prior call (if it anticipated continued acceleration) was directionally right on the print itself but the underlying momentum has already stalled. Core PCE remains the more telling read on persistence: it's basically flat at 1.19-1.20%, still 0.81pp below the 2% target, with no meaningful move in three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp). Core PCE shows no acceleration at all. Given headline's recent monthly prints are negative/flat and base effects from a year ago are now largely in the y/y number already, the room for further mechanical y/y acceleration is narrowing unless energy turns up again. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down on headline next print, with low-moderate conviction given base-effect uncertainty.
view.report: Headline CPI y/y has surged from near-zero to 1.24% over the last three prints, with the last two m/m readings (-0.11, -0.05) puzzling against that acceleration — the y/y jump is being driven by base effects (weak prints rolling off from a year ago) rather than fresh momentum. Indeed headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, essentially flat-to-falling on a recent-months basis, and headline_mom_3m_avg is -0.01%, both showing outgoing momentum is weaker than what's pushing the y/y rate up. This tells me the y/y acceleration is mostly a base-effect artifact of comparing against last year's energy-driven declines, and my prior call (if it anticipated continued acceleration) was directionally right on the print itself but the underlying momentum has already stalled. Core PCE remains the more telling read on persistence: it's basically flat at 1.19-1.20%, still 0.81pp below the 2% target, with no meaningful move in three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp). Core PCE shows no acceleration at all. Given headline's recent monthly prints are negative/flat and base effects from a year ago are now largely in the y/y number already, the room for further mechanical y/y acceleration is narrowing unless energy turns up again. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down on headline next print, with low-moderate conviction given base-effect uncertainty.
view.falsifier: A rebound in energy prices or a strong positive headline m/m print (>0.2%) would signal renewed upward momentum, not just base effects.

  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in December suggests that labor market conditions improved further even as economic growth slowed late last year.

  · Inflation is expected to remain low in the near term, in part because of the further declines in energy prices, but to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
  · The Committee is closely monitoring global economic and financial developments and is assessing their implications for the labor market and inflation, and for the balance of risks to the outlook.
  · Given the economic outlook, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

  · This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
  · Yellen, Chair; William C.
  · Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Stanley Fischer; Esther L.
  · George; Loretta J.
  · Mester; Jerome H.

  · Tarullo.

raw_response: {"report": "Last release confirmed a pullback: headline_mom came in at -0.13%, extending the softening from -0.11% and -0.05% the prior two months\u2014my \"flat\" call at 0.35 conviction was too neutral given this; the actual print showed deceleration in month/month terms even as the yoy figure dropped from 1.24 to 0.85, a sizeable -0.39pp swing. That yoy decline was driven by base effects rolling off a strong prior-year comparison rather than a genuine trend reversal, but the 3-month annualized headline is now -1.14%, clearly negative and the weakest momentum reading in the series. Core PCE ticked up to 1.41 from a 1.18-1.23 range, its highest print in the window, which is the one data point arguing for firming underlying pressure. But headline is what's being asked about, and headline's recent trajectory (three straight negative-to-flat mom prints, averaging -0.10%) points toward either a flat-to-lower yoy reading next month absent an energy rebound. The prior-year comparison base looks less favorable now (yoy is off its 1.24 high), meaning further base-effect declines are less likely, but recent mom weakness dominates the near-term signal. Net: momentum has clearly turned down even as core inflation firmed\u2014a divergence I weight toward headline's own momentum for this call.", "key_evidence": ["headline_mom", "headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_3m_annualized", "core_pce_yoy", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing"], "falsifier": "A strong positive headline m/m print (>0.2%), e.g. from energy rebound, would reverse this deceleration signal and imply renewed upward yoy momentum.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.45}

view.reasoning: Last release confirmed a pullback: headline_mom came in at -0.13%, extending the softening from -0.11% and -0.05% the prior two months—my "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was too neutral given this; the actual print showed deceleration in month/month terms even as the yoy figure dropped from 1.24 to 0.85, a sizeable -0.39pp swing. That yoy decline was driven by base effects rolling off a strong prior-year comparison rather than a genuine trend reversal, but the 3-month annualized headline is now -1.14%, clearly negative and the weakest momentum reading in the series. Core PCE ticked up to 1.41 from a 1.18-1.23 range, its highest print in the window, which is the one data point arguing for firming underlying pressure. But headline is what's being asked about, and headline's recent trajectory (three straight negative-to-flat mom prints, averaging -0.10%) points toward either a flat-to-lower yoy reading next month absent an energy rebound. The prior-year comparison base looks less favorable now (yoy is off its 1.24 high), meaning further base-effect declines are less likely, but recent mom weakness dominates the near-term signal. Net: momentum has clearly turned down even as core inflation firmed—a divergence I weight toward headline's own momentum for this call.

Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
Market-based measures of inflation compensation declined further; survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance, in recent months.
Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.

The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.

Inflation is expected to remain low in the near term, in part because of the further declines in energy prices, but to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
The Committee is closely monitoring global economic and financial developments and is assessing their implications for the labor market and inflation, and for the balance of risks to the outlook.
Given the economic outlook, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
Yellen, Chair; William C.
Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Stanley Fischer; Esther L.
George; Loretta J.
Mester; Jerome H.
Powell; Eric Rosengren; and Daniel K.
Tarullo.

view.reasoning: The prior call (down, 0.45) was wrong: headline y/y rose again to 0.89% from 0.85%, and the m/m print rebounded to +0.31%, exactly the kind of energy-led bounce I flagged as my falsifier. Headline y/y has climbed from -0.10% to 0.89% over the trailing year, with yoy_range_position at 0.74 showing we're in the upper part of the 12-month range, and headline_3m and yoy_change_3m still positive though headline_3m_annualized (+0.54%) is actually below the latest single-month pace, suggesting the m/m series is choppy rather than smoothly accelerating. Core PCE, the better persistence gauge, ticked up meaningfully in the last two prints (1.19→1.41→1.47), and core_3m_annualized (+1.67%) is running above the 12-month trend, indicating underlying inflation is also firming, not just energy-driven headline noise. Both series show synchronized upward momentum after a long soft patch, which is a more convincing signal than headline alone. Given the outgoing_mom of +0.10% is modest but the latest +0.31% print was strong, and core is genuinely turning up, I lean toward continued firming in headline y/y at the next release, though the mom_gap_vs_outgoing being slightly negative tempers conviction that this is a runaway trend.

view.reasoning: Last release: headline CPI yoy ticked up to 1.06% from 0.87% previous, with a 0.18% m/m print - a rebound after the prior soft -0.05% m/m reading. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given the underlying momentum; headline yoy has actually been range-bound between roughly 0.85-1.17% for the last five prints, so calling it flat wasn't wrong in spirit, but the m/m volatility (gap_vs_outgoing +0.36pp) shows headline is choppy print-to-print. Core PCE continues its slow, steady climb: 1.60% yoy, up from 1.18% thirteen months ago, and now at its highest point in this series, with core_yoy_change_3m at +0.08pp confirming gradual acceleration. Core PCE is the better read on persistence and it is unambiguously drifting higher, though still 0.40pp below the 2% target. Headline 3m annualized (1.66%) and core 3m annualized (1.69%) are similar and both below current yoy levels, suggesting the yoy pace may plateau or even ease slightly next month rather than extend the recent m/m bounce. Given headline's choppiness (one strong 0.18% print after a negative one) and core's steady but modest upward drift, I don't see strong evidence for another material yoy jump in headline CPI next month - more likely a small move either way within the recent range.

  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in September indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and growth of economic activity has picked up from the modest pace seen in the first half of this year.
  · Although the unemployment rate is little changed in recent months, job gains have been solid.
  · Household spending has been rising moderately but business fixed investment has remained soft.
  · Inflation has increased somewhat since earlier this year but is still below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
  · Market-based measures of inflation compensation have moved up but remain low; most survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance, in recent months.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
  · Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of past declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
  · Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
  · The Committee continues to closely monitor inflation indicators and global economic and financial developments.
  · Against this backdrop, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
  · The Committee judges that the case for an increase in the federal funds rate has continued to strengthen but decided, for the time being, to wait for some further evidence of continued progress toward its objectives.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
  · This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.

  · Tarullo.

unchanged: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in September indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and growth of economic activity has picked up from the modest pace seen in the first half of this year.
Although the unemployment rate is little changed in recent months, job gains have been solid.
Household spending has been rising moderately but business fixed investment has remained soft.

Inflation has increased somewhat since earlier this year but is still below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
Market-based measures of inflation compensation have moved up but remain low; most survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance, in recent months.
Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
The Committee expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of past declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
The Committee continues to closely monitor inflation indicators and global economic and financial developments.
Against this backdrop, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.

The Committee judges that the case for an increase in the federal funds rate has continued to strengthen but decided, for the time being, to wait for some further evidence of continued progress toward its objectives.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
Yellen, Chair; William C.
Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Stanley Fischer; Jerome H.
Powell; Eric Rosengren; and Daniel K.
Tarullo.
Voting against the action were: Esther L.
George and Loretta J.
Mester, each of whom preferred at this meeting to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in November indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and that economic activity has been expanding at a moderate pace since mid-year.
  · Job gains have been solid in recent months and the unemployment rate has declined.
  · Household spending has been rising moderately but business fixed investment has remained soft.
  · Inflation has increased since earlier this year but is still below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
  · Market-based measures of inflation compensation have moved up considerably but still are low; most survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance, in recent months.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
  · Inflation is expected to rise to 2 percent over the medium term as the transitory effects of past declines in energy and import prices dissipate and the labor market strengthens further.
  · Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
  · The Committee continues to closely monitor inflation indicators and global economic and financial developments.
  · In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · In light of the current shortfall of inflation from 2 percent, the Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected progress toward its inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
  · This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
  · Yellen, Chair; William C.
  · Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; James Bullard; Stanley Fischer; Esther L.
  · George; Loretta J.
  · Mester; Jerome H.
  · Powell; Eric Rosengren; and Daniel K.
  · Tarullo.

Job gains have been solid in recent months and the unemployment rate has declined.
Household spending has been rising moderately but business fixed investment has remained soft.

Inflation has increased since earlier this year but is still below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.

Market-based measures of inflation compensation have moved up considerably but still are low; most survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance, in recent months.
Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.

Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
The Committee continues to closely monitor inflation indicators and global economic and financial developments.
In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.

However, momentum is now decelerating: the outgoing m/m print (0.16%) is below the 3-month average (0.27%) and well off the 0.40% peak, and headline_3m_annualized (3.31%) is easing versus the y/y trend. Core PCE tells a much calmer story \u2014 up only 0.08pp over three months to 1.86%, still 0.14pp below the 2% target, with 3m annualized core running just 1.72%. This divergence (core_minus_headline -0.95pp) suggests the recent headline surge is being driven by volatile components (likely energy), not broad-based persistent inflation. I weight core PCE for persistence, and it shows only modest, steady drift toward target \u2014 not acceleration. Given the deceleration in the m/m pace of headline (0.16 vs 0.40 prior month) and gap vs outgoing trend, I expect headline y/y to keep rising slightly next month on base effects but at a slower pace, or stall near current levels. The strong y/y momentum of prior months looks to be fading at the margin.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "core_pce_yoy", "headline_mom", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing", "headline_3m_annualized", "core_3m_annualized", "core_minus_headline", "outgoing_mom"], "falsifier": "A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.30% would revive strong acceleration; a print near/below zero would confirm the stall and argue for flat/down.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "inflation_expectations", "why": "Survey/market-based inflation expectations would clarify if the headline rise is becoming embedded or remains transitory."}], "direction": "up", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.81% from 2.51%, continuing an eight-month climb from the 0.87% trough. However, momentum is now decelerating: the outgoing m/m print (0.16%) is below the 3-month average (0.27%) and well off the 0.40% peak, and headline_3m_annualized (3.31%) is easing versus the y/y trend. Core PCE tells a much calmer story — up only 0.08pp over three months to 1.86%, still 0.14pp below the 2% target, with 3m annualized core running just 1.72%. This divergence (core_minus_headline -0.95pp) suggests the recent headline surge is being driven by volatile components (likely energy), not broad-based persistent inflation. I weight core PCE for persistence, and it shows only modest, steady drift toward target — not acceleration. Given the deceleration in the m/m pace of headline (0.16 vs 0.40 prior month) and gap vs outgoing trend, I expect headline y/y to keep rising slightly next month on base effects but at a slower pace, or stall near current levels. The strong y/y momentum of prior months looks to be fading at the margin.
view.report: The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.81% from 2.51%, continuing an eight-month climb from the 0.87% trough. However, momentum is now decelerating: the outgoing m/m print (0.16%) is below the 3-month average (0.27%) and well off the 0.40% peak, and headline_3m_annualized (3.31%) is easing versus the y/y trend. Core PCE tells a much calmer story — up only 0.08pp over three months to 1.86%, still 0.14pp below the 2% target, with 3m annualized core running just 1.72%. This divergence (core_minus_headline -0.95pp) suggests the recent headline surge is being driven by volatile components (likely energy), not broad-based persistent inflation. I weight core PCE for persistence, and it shows only modest, steady drift toward target — not acceleration. Given the deceleration in the m/m pace of headline (0.16 vs 0.40 prior month) and gap vs outgoing trend, I expect headline y/y to keep rising slightly next month on base effects but at a slower pace, or stall near current levels. The strong y/y momentum of prior months looks to be fading at the margin.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.30% would revive strong acceleration; a print near/below zero would confirm the stall and argue for flat/down.

  · Market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low; survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee continues to expect that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace, and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
  · Inflation on a 12-month basis is expected to remain somewhat below 2 percent in the near term but to stabilize around the Committee's 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  · Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced, but the Committee is monitoring inflation developments closely.
  · In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1 to 1-1/4 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a sustained return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · The Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected inflation developments relative to its symmetric inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction.

view.reasoning: Last release confirms the "down" call: headline yoy ticked up slightly to 1.73% from 1.64%, but that's a modest bounce off the low, not a re-acceleration — my falsifier threshold (mom>0.30% or core PCE yoy>1.75%) was not breached. The recent trend is unambiguously disinflationary: headline yoy has fallen 0.45pp over 3 months and sits well off its 2.81% peak, now at only 0.38 of the way up the 12m range. The month-on-month prints are soft and decelerating — outgoing mom of 0.18% against a 3-month average of just 0.01%, and 3m annualized headline inflation is essentially flat at 0.08%. Core PCE also cooled 0.10pp over 3 months to 1.58%, comfortably below the 2% target and consistent with the Fed's own language that inflation is "running somewhat below 2 percent." The latest uptick to 1.73% from 1.64% is worth noting — it could mark a trough — but with mom momentum this weak (0.03% latest, negative two months prior), I don't see enough thrust for headline yoy to move meaningfully higher next release. I lean toward continued softness or a flat reading, tempered by the fact that the yoy series just posted its first uptick in four months.

The Committee continues to expect that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace, and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
Inflation on a 12-month basis is expected to remain somewhat below 2 percent in the near term but to stabilize around the Committee's 2 percent objective over the medium term.
Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced, but the Committee is monitoring inflation developments closely.
In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1 to 1-1/4 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a sustained return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
The Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected inflation developments relative to its symmetric inflation goal.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

For the time being, the Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction.

The Committee expects to begin implementing its balance sheet normalization program relatively soon, provided that the economy evolves broadly as anticipated; this program is described in the [date] Addendum to the Committee's Policy Normalization Principles and Plans.

Yellen, Chair; William C.
Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; Charles L.
Evans; Stanley Fischer; Patrick Harker; Robert S.
Kaplan; Neel Kashkari; and Jerome H.
Powell.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in September indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and that economic activity has been rising at a solid rate despite hurricane-related disruptions.
  · Although the hurricanes caused a drop in payroll employment in September, the unemployment rate declined further.
  · Household spending has been expanding at a moderate rate, and growth in business fixed investment has picked up in recent quarters.
  · Gasoline prices rose in the aftermath of the hurricanes, boosting overall inflation in September; however, inflation for items other than food and energy remained soft.
  · On a 12-month basis, both inflation measures have declined this year and are running below 2 percent.
  · Market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low; survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · Hurricane-related disruptions and rebuilding will continue to affect economic activity, employment, and inflation in the near term, but past experience suggests that the storms are unlikely to materially alter the course of the national economy over the medium term.
  · Consequently, the Committee continues to expect that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace, and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
  · Inflation on a 12-month basis is expected to remain somewhat below 2 percent in the near term but to stabilize around the Committee's 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  · Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced, but the Committee is monitoring inflation developments closely.
  · In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1 to 1-1/4 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a sustained return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · The Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected inflation developments relative to its symmetric inflation goal.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The balance sheet normalization program initiated in [date] is proceeding.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
  · Yellen, Chair; William C.
  · Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; Charles L.
  · Evans; Patrick Harker; Robert S.
  · Kaplan; Neel Kashkari; Jerome H.
  · Powell; and Randal K.
  · Quarles.

unchanged: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in September indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and that economic activity has been rising at a solid rate despite hurricane-related disruptions.
Although the hurricanes caused a drop in payroll employment in September, the unemployment rate declined further.
Household spending has been expanding at a moderate rate, and growth in business fixed investment has picked up in recent quarters.
Gasoline prices rose in the aftermath of the hurricanes, boosting overall inflation in September; however, inflation for items other than food and energy remained soft.
On a 12-month basis, both inflation measures have declined this year and are running below 2 percent.
Market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low; survey-based measures of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance.
Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.

Hurricane-related disruptions and rebuilding will continue to affect economic activity, employment, and inflation in the near term, but past experience suggests that the storms are unlikely to materially alter the course of the national economy over the medium term.

Consequently, the Committee continues to expect that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace, and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
Inflation on a 12-month basis is expected to remain somewhat below 2 percent in the near term but to stabilize around the Committee's 2 percent objective over the medium term.
Near-term risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced, but the Committee is monitoring inflation developments closely.
In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1 to 1-1/4 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting some further strengthening in labor market conditions and a sustained return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
The Committee will carefully monitor actual and expected inflation developments relative to its symmetric inflation goal.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
The balance sheet normalization program initiated in [date] is proceeding.
Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.
Yellen, Chair; William C.
Dudley, Vice Chairman; Lael Brainard; Charles L.
Evans; Patrick Harker; Robert S.
Kaplan; Neel Kashkari; Jerome H.
Powell; and Randal K.
Quarles.

raw_response: {"report": "The last print (2.17% y/y headline, +0.27% m/m) came in above the outgoing pace and above my 0.25% trigger, confirming the reacceleration I flagged as a risk rather than continued fade \u2014 so my prior \"flat\" call undersold the momentum shift. Headline y/y has now risen for three straight months (1.93\u21922.18\u21922.02\u21922.17), the 3m annualized rate is 3.47% versus a 12-month-trailing y/y of ~2.17%, and yoy_change_3m is +0.24pp \u2014 clear upward momentum in headline, likely still energy/gasoline driven per the hurricane-related commentary. Core PCE also ticked up last month (1.42\u21921.54) after eight months of decline, though it remains 0.46pp below target and the 3m annualized core pace (1.92%) is still soft, so the core signal is much more muted \u2014 I weight core less here because it's a single-month uptick against a longer downtrend, likely noise or partial pass-through. Given headline's clear multi-month acceleration and mom_gap_vs_outgoing being slightly positive, I expect headline CPI y/y to be higher again next month, though the gap between core's continued softness and headline's energy-driven pop tempers my confidence that this is durable.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "headline_3m_annualized", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "core_pce_yoy", "core_3m_annualized", "outgoing_mom", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing"], "falsifier": "A headline m/m print back below ~0.10% or a renewed core PCE decline would suggest the recent uptick was energy-driven noise, not genuine acceleration.", "missing_inputs": [], "direction": "up", "conviction": 0.45}

view.reasoning: The last print (2.17% y/y headline, +0.27% m/m) came in above the outgoing pace and above my 0.25% trigger, confirming the reacceleration I flagged as a risk rather than continued fade — so my prior "flat" call undersold the momentum shift. Headline y/y has now risen for three straight months (1.93→2.18→2.02→2.17), the 3m annualized rate is 3.47% versus a 12-month-trailing y/y of ~2.17%, and yoy_change_3m is +0.24pp — clear upward momentum in headline, likely still energy/gasoline driven per the hurricane-related commentary. Core PCE also ticked up last month (1.42→1.54) after eight months of decline, though it remains 0.46pp below target and the 3m annualized core pace (1.92%) is still soft, so the core signal is much more muted — I weight core less here because it's a single-month uptick against a longer downtrend, likely noise or partial pass-through. Given headline's clear multi-month acceleration and mom_gap_vs_outgoing being slightly positive, I expect headline CPI y/y to be higher again next month, though the gap between core's continued softness and headline's energy-driven pop tempers my confidence that this is durable.
view.report: The last print (2.17% y/y headline, +0.27% m/m) came in above the outgoing pace and above my 0.25% trigger, confirming the reacceleration I flagged as a risk rather than continued fade — so my prior "flat" call undersold the momentum shift. Headline y/y has now risen for three straight months (1.93→2.18→2.02→2.17), the 3m annualized rate is 3.47% versus a 12-month-trailing y/y of ~2.17%, and yoy_change_3m is +0.24pp — clear upward momentum in headline, likely still energy/gasoline driven per the hurricane-related commentary. Core PCE also ticked up last month (1.42→1.54) after eight months of decline, though it remains 0.46pp below target and the 3m annualized core pace (1.92%) is still soft, so the core signal is much more muted — I weight core less here because it's a single-month uptick against a longer downtrend, likely noise or partial pass-through. Given headline's clear multi-month acceleration and mom_gap_vs_outgoing being slightly positive, I expect headline CPI y/y to be higher again next month, though the gap between core's continued softness and headline's energy-driven pop tempers my confidence that this is durable.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print back below ~0.10% or a renewed core PCE decline would suggest the recent uptick was energy-driven noise, not genuine acceleration.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

Kaplan; Jerome H.
Powell; and Randal K.
Quarles.
Voting against the action were Charles L.
Evans and Neel Kashkari, who preferred at this meeting to maintain the existing target range for the federal funds rate.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in May indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and that economic activity has been rising at a solid rate.
  · Job gains have been strong, on average, in recent months, and the unemployment rate has declined.
  · Recent data suggest that growth of household spending has picked up, while business fixed investment has continued to grow strongly.
  · On a 12-month basis, both overall inflation and inflation for items other than food and energy have moved close to 2 percent.
  · Indicators of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee expects that further gradual increases in the target range for the federal funds rate will be consistent with sustained expansion of economic activity, strong labor market conditions, and inflation near the Committee's symmetric 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  · Risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
  · In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1-3/4 to 2 percent.
  · The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting strong labor market conditions and a sustained return to 2 percent inflation.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its maximum employment objective and its symmetric 2 percent inflation objective.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were Jerome H.
  · Powell, Chairman; William C.
  · Dudley, Vice Chairman; Thomas I.
  · Barkin; Raphael W.
  · Bostic; Lael Brainard; Loretta J.
  · Mester; Randal K.
  · Quarles; and John C.
  · Williams.

However, the most recent m/m print (0.21) is slightly above the 3-month average (0.15) and above the outgoing month's 0.08, which tempers the deceleration signal somewhat \u2014 a base-effect-driven snapback in y/y is not obviously loading up, but the momentum isn't uniformly soft either. Core PCE's deceleration is milder (only -0.09pp over 3 months) and sits near target distance of -0.10pp, suggesting core price pressure has largely normalized rather than continuing to fall sharply. Net: further downside is likely but with less room to run than the last two prints suggested, since core is now close to target and headline momentum ticked up slightly last month.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "core_pce_yoy", "core_yoy_change_3m", "headline_mom_3m_avg", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing", "yoy_range_position", "core_distance_from_target"], "falsifier": "A headline m/m print above 0.30% combined with core PCE yoy rising back above 2.00% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued cooling.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "labor_tightness", "why": "Would clarify if wage pressures are feeding into any inflation reacceleration risk."}, {"driver": "inflation_expectations", "why": "Would confirm whether the disinflation trend is anchored or at risk of reversing."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning:
The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 2.85 to 2.64 to 2.33 over the last two prints, a sharp deceleration, and the most recent m/m reading (0.18) was unremarkable while the newest 0.21 print keeps the 3m annualized pace subdued at 1.87%. Core PCE also ticked down from 2.00 to 1.90, reinforcing that the underlying trend cooled rather than accelerated. My falsifier condition (m/m above 0.35% with core PCE above 2.05%) did not trigger, so the disinflation call held.

Looking ahead one month: headline y/y has now dropped 0.48pp over three months and sits well off its 12-month high (2.85), with yoy_range_position at just 0.37 — closer to the low end of the recent range. However, the most recent m/m print (0.21) is slightly above the 3-month average (0.15) and above the outgoing month's 0.08, which tempers the deceleration signal somewhat — a base-effect-driven snapback in y/y is not obviously loading up, but the momentum isn't uniformly soft either. Core PCE's deceleration is milder (only -0.09pp over 3 months) and sits near target distance of -0.10pp, suggesting core price pressure has largely normalized rather than continuing to fall sharply. Net: further downside is likely but with less room to run than the last two prints suggested, since core is now close to target and headline momentum ticked up slightly last month.
view.report:
The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 2.85 to 2.64 to 2.33 over the last two prints, a sharp deceleration, and the most recent m/m reading (0.18) was unremarkable while the newest 0.21 print keeps the 3m annualized pace subdued at 1.87%. Core PCE also ticked down from 2.00 to 1.90, reinforcing that the underlying trend cooled rather than accelerated. My falsifier condition (m/m above 0.35% with core PCE above 2.05%) did not trigger, so the disinflation call held.

Looking ahead one month: headline y/y has now dropped 0.48pp over three months and sits well off its 12-month high (2.85), with yoy_range_position at just 0.37 — closer to the low end of the recent range. However, the most recent m/m print (0.21) is slightly above the 3-month average (0.15) and above the outgoing month's 0.08, which tempers the deceleration signal somewhat — a base-effect-driven snapback in y/y is not obviously loading up, but the momentum isn't uniformly soft either. Core PCE's deceleration is milder (only -0.09pp over 3 months) and sits near target distance of -0.10pp, suggesting core price pressure has largely normalized rather than continuing to fall sharply. Net: further downside is likely but with less room to run than the last two prints suggested, since core is now close to target and headline momentum ticked up slightly last month.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print above 0.30% combined with core PCE yoy rising back above 2.00% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued cooling.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in August indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and that economic activity has been rising at a strong rate.
  · Job gains have been strong, on average, in recent months, and the unemployment rate has stayed low.
  · Household spending and business fixed investment have grown strongly.
  · On a 12-month basis, both overall inflation and inflation for items other than food and energy remain near 2 percent.
  · Indicators of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance.
  · Consistent with its statutory mandate, the Committee seeks to foster maximum employment and price stability.
  · The Committee expects that further gradual increases in the target range for the federal funds rate will be consistent with sustained expansion of economic activity, strong labor market conditions, and inflation near the Committee's symmetric 2 percent objective over the medium term.
  · Risks to the economic outlook appear roughly balanced.
  · In view of realized and expected labor market conditions and inflation, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 2 to 2-1/4 percent.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its maximum employment objective and its symmetric 2 percent inflation objective.
  · This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Jerome H.
  · Powell, Chairman; John C.
  · Williams, Vice Chairman; Thomas I.
  · Barkin; Raphael W.
  · Bostic; Lael Brainard; Richard H.
  · Clarida; Esther L.
  · George; Loretta J.
  · Mester; and Randal K.
  · Quarles.

view.reasoning: Last release (2.33) reversed sharply higher to 2.49 y/y, so my prior "down" call was wrong — the dip to 2.33 was a pause, not the start of sustained deceleration. Headline mom printed 0.23, roughly in line with the 3m average (0.21) and modestly above the outgoing print, consistent with the bounce. Core PCE also ticked up to 1.96 from 1.90, still just shy of 2% but within the recent 1.88-2.00 band it has occupied for several months — no clear trend either way at the margin, though it remains close to target with core_yoy_change_3m essentially flat (+0.02pp). Headline's 3m annualized (2.50%) is running above core's (1.31%), and the yoy series sits mid-range (0.50 position) between its 12m low and high, having just moved off the low. The bounce back to 2.49 combined with a firming core suggests the deceleration trend has stalled rather than reversing into a clear new acceleration. With headline mom of 0.23 not exceeding my 0.30 threshold, and core PCE at 1.96 not clearing 2.00, my stated reversal condition is not fully met, but the direction of travel in this print is clearly upward. I lean toward continued firmness rather than resumed cooling next month.

raw_response: {"report": "My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: headline CPI yoy fell sharply from 2.49 to 2.15, and the newest headline_mom printed at -0.07%, well below my 0.15% falsifier threshold, alongside core PCE yoy slipping to 1.87 (also below my 1.90 threshold). view.reasoning: My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: headline CPI yoy fell sharply from 2.49 to 2.15, and the newest headline_mom printed at -0.07%, well below my 0.15% falsifier threshold, alongside core PCE yoy slipping to 1.87 (also below my 1.90 threshold). Both conditions I set for reversing my view were met. The yoy_range_position of 0.02 shows headline sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range, and headline_yoy_change_3m of -0.50pp confirms a clear downward trend over the last quarter. Core PCE, which I weight more for persistence, has also rolled over modestly (core_yoy_change_3m -0.12pp) after a run-up, suggesting the strengthening trend has stalled. Energy/food-driven noise could explain part of the headline drop (the -0.07% mom print), but the softening is corroborated by core weakness too. Given a single sharp negative print can partly mean-revert next month, I don't have full conviction in further declines, but the weight of evidence favors continued softness or at least no reacceleration.

view.reasoning: The prior "down" call was directionally right for one more month: headline CPI yoy fell from 2.15 to 2.00, continuing the deceleration trend, and the last mom print of 0.07% was soft. However, my stated flip condition was partially triggered - core PCE yoy printed 2.01%, above my 1.95% threshold, even though headline mom (0.07%) stayed well below the 0.25% trigger. This is a mixed signal: headline yoy has now fallen to the 12-month low (2.00%) with yoy_change_3m at -0.33pp, but core PCE is drifting up to 2.01% (yoy_change_3m +0.11pp) and core 3m annualized is running hot at 2.13% versus headline's 0.93%. That divergence matters - core is the better persistence read, and it's showing renewed firmness even as energy/food-driven headline cools. The single soft mom print (0.07%) after a slightly negative one (-0.07%) looks like it could be transitory rather than a firm trend, especially given core's momentum is opposite. Given headline sits exactly at its 12-month low and any mean-reversion combined with core firming, I lean toward a bounce or flattening in headline yoy next month rather than further decline. Conviction is modest given the softness in recent mom prints.

view.reasoning:
The latest print confirms a sharp deceleration in headline, not the flat/up outcome I expected last time. Headline CPI y/y fell to 1.49% from 2.00%, a large 0.51pp drop, with the newest mom reading at -0.08% — the second negative m/m print in three months. Headline 3m annualized is now -0.33%, and yoy has fallen 1.00pp over three months, moving from mid-range to the bottom of its 12-month band (yoy_range_position = 0). My prior flat call was wrong: I underweighted the momentum signal (outgoing_mom already softening, mom_gap_vs_outgoing negative) and treated the yoy stability as more durable than it was.

Core PCE tells a different story: still drifting up modestly, 2.04% last, 3m annualized +2.09%, sitting essentially at target and up 0.08pp over three months. This divergence (core_minus_headline = +0.55pp) suggests the headline drop is being driven by energy/food base effects or one-off softness rather than broad disinflation in underlying prices.

For the one-month-ahead headline call, the momentum is clearly negative — two of the last three m/m prints were negative, and the yoy trend has been falling for three straight months into a 12-month low. Absent a snapback in energy, headline is more likely to stay low or drift lower than to rebound sharply. I'll weight the momentum here since it's the near-term forecast question, while noting core remains stable near target.

Headline 3m annualized is only 1.16%, and core 3m annualized is 1.61% — both comfortably below the 2% target, with core PCE yoy at 1.64%, essentially flat over the last three prints (1.56, 1.66, 1.64) and still 0.36pp below target. The yoy_range_position of 0.25 shows headline sitting in the lower quartile of its 12-month range. Core, which I weight more heavily for persistence since it strips volatile food/energy, shows no sign of re-acceleration — it's been rangebound in the 1.6-1.7% band for several months after drifting down from ~2.0%. Headline's single-month uptick to 1.83% before this reversal now looks like noise, not a trend change. With soft m/m readings, subdued 3m annualized rates, and core PCE stalled below target, the disinflationary/flat regime looks intact rather than reversing upward. I'm revising down from my prior "up" call.

Headline 3m annualized is only 1.16%, and core 3m annualized is 1.61% — both comfortably below the 2% target, with core PCE yoy at 1.64%, essentially flat over the last three prints (1.56, 1.66, 1.64) and still 0.36pp below target. The yoy_range_position of 0.25 shows headline sitting in the lower quartile of its 12-month range. Core, which I weight more heavily for persistence since it strips volatile food/energy, shows no sign of re-acceleration — it's been rangebound in the 1.6-1.7% band for several months after drifting down from ~2.0%. Headline's single-month uptick to 1.83% before this reversal now looks like noise, not a trend change. With soft m/m readings, subdued 3m annualized rates, and core PCE stalled below target, the disinflationary/flat regime looks intact rather than reversing upward. I'm revising down from my prior "up" call.

view.reasoning: Last release: headline CPI yoy ticked down to 1.68% from 1.74%, with the latest print's momentum (0.15% m/m) below the outgoing pace (0.23%) but above the 3m average — my prior "flat" call was roughly right in direction (headline drifted marginally lower, not sharply higher or lower), though I noted a trigger for acceleration that wasn't met (mom stayed below 0.25%, core PCE yoy at 1.75% is right at the threshold I flagged, not clearly above it). Core PCE yoy actually rose from 1.64% to 1.75%, its best reading in three months, and the 3m annualized core pace (1.66%) still sits below the 12-month trend, but the last two prints show a modest uptick. Headline's yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (+0.01pp) while core's is up +0.18pp — a mild divergence where core is showing incipient firming while headline is anchored by soft momentum. Given headline_mom_3m_avg (0.16%) annualizes to under 2%, and the outgoing print decelerated from the prior month, I don't see strong evidence for a meaningful headline acceleration next month. The Committee expects to maintain this target range until it is confident that the economy has weathered recent events and is on track to achieve its maximum employment and price stability goals.
This action will help support economic activity, strong labor market conditions, and inflation returning to the Committee's symmetric 2 percent objective.

The Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook, including information related to public health, as well as global developments and muted inflation pressures, and will use its tools and act as appropriate to support the economy.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the stance of monetary policy, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its maximum employment objective and its symmetric 2 percent inflation objective.

This assessment will take into account a wide range of information, including measures of labor market conditions, indicators of inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and readings on financial and international developments.
The Federal Reserve is prepared to use its full range of tools to support the flow of credit to households and businesses and thereby promote its maximum employment and price stability goals.

To support the smooth functioning of markets for Treasury securities and agency mortgage-backed securities that are central to the flow of credit to households and businesses, over coming months the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $500 billion and its holdings of agency mortgage-backed securities by at least $200 billion.
The Committee will also reinvest all principal payments from the Federal Reserve's holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities.
In addition, the Open Market Desk has recently expanded its overnight and term repurchase agreement operations.
The Committee will continue to closely monitor market conditions and is prepared to adjust its plans as appropriate.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H.
Powell, Chair; John C.
Williams, Vice Chair; Michelle W.
Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H.
Clarida; Patrick Harker; Robert S.
Kaplan; Neel Kashkari; and Randal K.
Quarles.
Voting against this action was Loretta J.

Mester, who was fully supportive of all of the actions taken to promote the smooth functioning of markets and the flow of credit to households and businesses but preferred to reduce the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent at this meeting.

In a related set of actions to support the credit needs of households and businesses, the Federal Reserve announced measures related to the discount window, intraday credit, bank capital and liquidity buffers, reserve requirements, and—in coordination with other central banks—the U.S.
dollar liquidity swap line arrangements.
More information can be found on the Federal Reserve Board's website .
