view.report: This is clearly the COVID demand-shock collapse: headline CPI y/y has cratered from 2.60% to 0.20% over four prints, with the last two months showing outright deflation (-0.45%, -0.79% m/m) before a smaller -0.09% m/m in the latest month. My prior "down" call was correct — the trough continued and even deepened past what I anticipated, with headline_3m_annualized at -5.2%. However, the falsifier I set (m/m back to +0.1% or higher) was not met, but the pace of decline decelerated sharply: outgoing_mom of -0.09% is far less negative than the -0.79% two months prior. This is the classic bottoming pattern — the massive negative print is rolling out of the y/y calculation, and the m/m deflation is fading toward flat. Core PCE also cratered to 1.00% from ~1.55-1.70% typical range, confirming broad-based demand destruction, not just energy. Given base effects (a small positive or flat headline print now compares against a much smaller prior-year base than the collapsing comparisons of recent months) and the clear deceleration in the pace of monthly declines, I expect the y/y rate to stabilize or tick up modestly rather than fall further. The extreme deflationary impulse looks to be exhausting itself.
view.falsifier: Another sharp negative m/m print (below -0.3%) or core_pce_yoy falling further below 1.00% would signal continued disinflationary momentum, not stabilization.

missing_inputs.why: Would clarify if the deflationary shock is expected to be transitory (energy/demand) or embedding into forward pricing.

view.reasoning:
Last release: headline yoy ticked down to 1.18% from 1.23%, with m/m of 0.23%, a mild rebound from the prior month's 0.12% but still below the 0.29% print two months back. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong on direction for headline yoy (it fell, not rose), though the m/m did tick up slightly rather than stalling further — a partial vindication of the underlying momentum thesis but not the yoy call itself.

Looking at the full picture: headline yoy has been drifting down for three straight months (1.39→1.23→1.18), and the 3m annualized headline pace (2.48%) is now running below the most recent single m/m print's implied trend, suggesting base effects are still working against yoy even as monthly prints are modestly firming (mom_gap_vs_outgoing is only -0.09pp, m/m stabilizing around 0.2-0.25%). Core PCE yoy, by contrast, has been climbing for three months (1.21→1.39→1.48→1.42, last tick down slightly) and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.21pp — a clearer acceleration signal, though it dipped last month too. Core sits 0.58pp below target, still with room to run higher before mattering for headline read-through.

Given headline's recent monthly prints (0.12, 0.23) are unremarkable and yoy math still has favorable base effects rolling off, I lean toward another flat-to-slightly-down headline yoy print, with core's modest deceleration tempering conviction in either direction.

view.reasoning: The last print vindicates the acceleration risk I flagged: headline m/m came in at 0.43%, above my 0.35% flip threshold, pushing headline yoy to 1.32% from 1.18% and lifting the 3-month annualized pace to 3.19% — well above the prior trend and above target. Core PCE yoy also ticked up to 1.45% from 1.42%, continuing a steady climb from the 0.97% trough several months back, though its 3m annualized pace (1.38%) is still muted and below headline's recent pace, suggesting some of the headline pop is energy/food-driven rather than pure core persistence. Still, the momentum in both series is now consistently upward for several consecutive prints (core yoy has risen 5 of the last 6 months), and headline mom has stepped up from ~0.12-0.26% into the 0.4%+ range twice in the last three months. Given headline is still below its 12-month high (2.60%) but clearly off the 0.20% trough and accelerating on a 3-month basis, I read this as a genuine, if early-stage, reacceleration rather than noise. I weight the recent momentum more than the low absolute level, since two consecutive strong mom prints out of three is a meaningful signal, not a one-off.
view.report: The last print vindicates the acceleration risk I flagged: headline m/m came in at 0.43%, above my 0.35% flip threshold, pushing headline yoy to 1.32% from 1.18% and lifting the 3-month annualized pace to 3.19% — well above the prior trend and above target. Core PCE yoy also ticked up to 1.45% from 1.42%, continuing a steady climb from the 0.97% trough several months back, though its 3m annualized pace (1.38%) is still muted and below headline's recent pace, suggesting some of the headline pop is energy/food-driven rather than pure core persistence. Still, the momentum in both series is now consistently upward for several consecutive prints (core yoy has risen 5 of the last 6 months), and headline mom has stepped up from ~0.12-0.26% into the 0.4%+ range twice in the last three months. Given headline is still below its 12-month high (2.60%) but clearly off the 0.20% trough and accelerating on a 3-month basis, I read this as a genuine, if early-stage, reacceleration rather than noise. I weight the recent momentum more than the low absolute level, since two consecutive strong mom prints out of three is a meaningful signal, not a one-off.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print back below 0.15% or core PCE yoy stalling/reversing below 1.4% would undercut the reacceleration read.

  · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S.
  · economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
  · The COVID-19 pandemic is causing tremendous human and economic hardship across the United States and around the world.
  · The pace of the recovery in economic activity and employment has moderated in recent months, with weakness concentrated in the sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic.
  · Weaker demand and earlier declines in oil prices have been holding down consumer price inflation.
  · Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S.
  · households and businesses.
  · The path of the economy will depend significantly on the course of the virus, including progress on vaccinations.
  · The ongoing public health crisis continues to weigh on economic activity, employment, and inflation, and poses considerable risks to the economic outlook.
  · The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run.
  · With inflation running persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longer‑term inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent.
  · The Committee expects to maintain an accommodative stance of monetary policy until these outcomes are achieved.
  · The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment and inflation has risen to 2 percent and is on track to moderately exceed 2 percent for some time.
  · In addition, the Federal Reserve will continue to increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $80 billion per month and of agency mortgage‑backed securities by at least $40 billion per month until substantial further progress has been made toward the Committee's maximum employment and price stability goals.
  · These asset purchases help foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
  · In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.
  · The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals.
  · The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

unchanged: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] The Federal Reserve is committed to using its full range of tools to support the U.S.
economy in this challenging time, thereby promoting its maximum employment and price stability goals.
The COVID-19 pandemic is causing tremendous human and economic hardship across the United States and around the world.
The pace of the recovery in economic activity and employment has moderated in recent months, with weakness concentrated in the sectors most adversely affected by the pandemic.
Weaker demand and earlier declines in oil prices have been holding down consumer price inflation.
Overall financial conditions remain accommodative, in part reflecting policy measures to support the economy and the flow of credit to U.S.
households and businesses.
The path of the economy will depend significantly on the course of the virus, including progress on vaccinations.
The ongoing public health crisis continues to weigh on economic activity, employment, and inflation, and poses considerable risks to the economic outlook.
The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run.

With inflation running persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longer‑term inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent.
The Committee expects to maintain an accommodative stance of monetary policy until these outcomes are achieved.

The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and expects it will be appropriate to maintain this target range until labor market conditions have reached levels consistent with the Committee's assessments of maximum employment and inflation has risen to 2 percent and is on track to moderately exceed 2 percent for some time.

In addition, the Federal Reserve will continue to increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $80 billion per month and of agency mortgage‑backed securities by at least $40 billion per month until substantial further progress has been made toward the Committee's maximum employment and price stability goals.
These asset purchases help foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.
The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals.

The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on public health, labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.
Voting for the monetary policy action were Jerome H.

Williams, Vice Chair; Thomas I.

Bostic; Michelle W.
Bowman; Lael Brainard; Richard H.
Clarida; Mary C.

Waller.

Momentum indicators confirm broad-based acceleration, not a one-off: headline 3m annualized is 4.53% vs the 2.67% y/y print, meaning the annualized trend is running above even the newly-elevated y/y rate, implying further y/y increases are likely mechanically baked in as base effects catch up.   · households and businesses.

  · The ongoing public health crisis continues to weigh on the economy, and risks to the economic outlook remain.
  · The Committee seeks to achieve maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run.
  · With inflation running persistently below this longer-run goal, the Committee will aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time so that inflation averages 2 percent over time and longer‑term inflation expectations remain well anchored at 2 percent.

  · In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.
  · The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals.

raw_response: {"report": "Last release proved my \"flat\" call wrong: headline CPI yoy rose to 5.35% from 5.15%, a second consecutive uptick, and the mom print came in at 0.45%, above the outgoing 0.28% and above my 0.3% down-trigger \u2014 so the genuine acceleration case, not the flat one, was validated. Core PCE yoy also climbed again to 3.96% from 3.93%, continuing an unbroken multi-month climb and still nowhere near turning over. The 3m annualized rates for both headline (4.90%) and core (4.94%) sit well above the trailing yoy figures, confirming momentum has not decayed \u2014 this is not just base effects. view.reasoning: The previous "up" call is confirmed and then some. Headline CPI y/y jumped from 5.35 to 6.24, a full 0.89pp acceleration, driven by the outgoing MoM print surging to 0.96% (vs 0.45% prior and a 0.57% 3m average) — well above the 0.3% threshold that would have signaled a turn, not below it. Core PCE also continued climbing, 3.96→4.01, its ninth consecutive monthly increase in the series shown, though the pace of core acceleration has clearly decelerated (core_yoy_change_3m only +0.15pp vs headline's +0.99pp), suggesting the recent surge is more headline/energy-food driven. Still, the 3m annualized headline pace of 7.01% is far hotter than the already-elevated 12m trend, and core's 3m annualized 3.88% keeps it well above any deceleration signal. Level-wise both are far from target (core 2.01pp above), and yoy_range_position sits at the top of its 12-month range with no sign of curvature. Policy language describes inflation as "transitory," but the data flatly contradicts that characterization — there is no evidence of deceleration momentum anywhere in these prints. Given the sharp MoM re-acceleration, another upside headline print next month is the more probable outcome, though core's slower pace of increase tempers full conviction.

view.reasoning:
The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89% — both accelerating further, not decelerating. My falsifier condition (sub-0.3% MoM plus falling core PCE) did not occur; instead outgoing_mom was 0.34% and core PCE kept rising, confirming the acceleration remains intact for now.

That said, there are early signs of the pace cresting rather than reaccelerating outright. Headline MoM prints have been volatile (0.96, 0.86, 0.69, 0.60) — the last three months show deceleration in the monthly pace even as the y/y comparison keeps climbing due to base effects. The 3m annualized headline (8.95%) is still very hot and above the y/y rate, meaning y/y has more room to rise before it decelerates, but the trend in MoM readings (down from 0.96 to 0.60) suggests the sequential impulse is easing. Core PCE's 3m annualized (6.64%) remains well above target and above trailing y/y, meaning further core y/y increases are still baked in for at least one more print. Given the mom deceleration hasn't yet been strong enough to break the y/y trend, and 3m annualized readings still exceed y/y in both series, I expect headline CPI y/y to rise again next month, though the momentum deceleration tempers my confidence versus last time.
view.report:
The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89% — both accelerating further, not decelerating. My falsifier condition (sub-0.3% MoM plus falling core PCE) did not occur; instead outgoing_mom was 0.34% and core PCE kept rising, confirming the acceleration remains intact for now.

That said, there are early signs of the pace cresting rather than reaccelerating outright. Headline MoM prints have been volatile (0.96, 0.86, 0.69, 0.60) — the last three months show deceleration in the monthly pace even as the y/y comparison keeps climbing due to base effects. The 3m annualized headline (8.95%) is still very hot and above the y/y rate, meaning y/y has more room to rise before it decelerates, but the trend in MoM readings (down from 0.96 to 0.60) suggests the sequential impulse is easing. Core PCE's 3m annualized (6.64%) remains well above target and above trailing y/y, meaning further core y/y increases are still baked in for at least one more print. Given the mom deceleration hasn't yet been strong enough to break the y/y trend, and 3m annualized readings still exceed y/y in both series, I expect headline CPI y/y to rise again next month, though the momentum deceleration tempers my confidence versus last time.
view.falsifier: Two consecutive months of headline MoM below 0.3% alongside core PCE y/y turning down would confirm the acceleration has broken.

raw_response: {"report": "The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 7.94% from 7.56%, extending an unbroken acceleration visible across the full 13-month series. Momentum remains hot, not cooling: the outgoing MoM print was 0.69%, above the 3-month average of 0.66%, and the 3-month annualized headline pace (8.25%) is running faster than the y/y rate itself, meaning the trend is still steepening rather than plateauing. Core PCE tells the same story and is the more important read for persistence \u2014 it has climbed every single month of the series with no exception, now at 5.36% y/y versus 4.44% three months ago (+0.92pp), and shows no sign of turning down. Both headline and core distance from the 2% target are large and widening (core now 3.36pp above target).   · [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen.

  · In support of these goals, the Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent.
  · With inflation well above 2 percent and a strong labor market, the Committee expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range for the federal funds rate.
  · The Committee decided to continue to reduce the monthly pace of its net asset purchases, bringing them to an end in early March.
  · Beginning in February, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $20 billion per month and of agency mortgage‑backed securities by at least $10 billion per month.
  · The Federal Reserve's ongoing purchases and holdings of securities will continue to foster smooth market functioning and accommodative financial conditions, thereby supporting the flow of credit to households and businesses.
  · In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.

Energy/gasoline swings likely explain much of the headline m/m collapse, but that is exactly the kind of base effect that can persist for a print or two given how large a role energy played in the run-up. Given the outgoing_mom near zero and core roughly flat at an elevated level, next month's headline y/y is more likely to decelerate further or hold near current levels than resume climbing toward new highs.", "key_evidence": ["headline_cpi_yoy", "headline_mom", "outgoing_mom", "core_pce_yoy", "core_yoy_change_3m", "headline_yoy_change_3m", "mom_gap_vs_outgoing"], "falsifier": "A rebound in headline m/m back above 0.6% alongside core PCE y/y climbing above 5.5% would signal reacceleration, not deceleration.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "labor_tightness", "why": "Wage growth momentum would clarify if core services inflation is likely to persist or ease."}, {"driver": "financial_conditions", "why": "Energy/commodity price trends would clarify whether the headline m/m dip is a one-off or durable."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.55}

My previous "up" call at 0.60 conviction was wrong; the June-August acceleration did not extend into this print, and headline actually reversed. Core PCE y/y also ticked up slightly to 5.32% from 5.18%, but this follows two consecutive months of decline (5.60→5.59→5.35→5.18) before this small uptick, so the core trend is still best read as stalling/rolling over rather than reaccelerating — core_yoy_change_3m is -0.28pp over the quarter. The 3m annualized headline pace of 9.10% is stale, driven by the volatile mid-year spike months rolling off; the single-month behavior (-0.01% m/m) is the more telling signal for near-term momentum. Energy/gasoline swings likely explain much of the headline m/m collapse, but that is exactly the kind of base effect that can persist for a print or two given how large a role energy played in the run-up. Given the outgoing_mom near zero and core roughly flat at an elevated level, next month's headline y/y is more likely to decelerate further or hold near current levels than resume climbing toward new highs.

Energy/gasoline swings likely explain much of the headline m/m collapse, but that is exactly the kind of base effect that can persist for a print or two given how large a role energy played in the run-up. Given the outgoing_mom near zero and core roughly flat at an elevated level, next month's headline y/y is more likely to decelerate further or hold near current levels than resume climbing toward new highs.

view.reasoning: The prior "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 4.92% from 5.96%, continuing the steady deceleration seen over the last 13 months (from 8.57% down to 4.92%). The most recent m/m print of 0.12% is well below the 3m average of 0.33% and the outgoing 0.31%, and headline 3m annualized (4.06%) sits below the y/y rate, confirming disinflation momentum rather than a plateau. Core PCE is decelerating too, though much more slowly and from a stickier level (5.60% → 4.86%, still 2.86pp above target), with core 3m annualized (4.86%) roughly matching its y/y pace — persistence remains the story here. The gap between core and headline has essentially closed (core_minus_headline -0.06pp), meaning base effects that drove the sharp headline decline are largely exhausted, so further headline drops will need to come from actual monthly disinflation rather than falling off high year-ago comparisons. Given the very low latest m/m (0.12%) and continued high base compares still rolling off, I lean toward another modest decline in headline y/y at the next release, but with less confidence than before since the easy base-effect gains are fading and core remains sticky, limiting how much further headline can fall.

view.reasoning: Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked up to 3.29% from 3.07%, the first uptick after a long disinflation streak — my prior "down" call was wrong for that print. The rise was driven by a base effect (12m-low comparison from a very low prior-year print) rather than a fresh acceleration in momentum: m/m prints have been running 0.12-0.22% for the last several months, well below the 0.3-0.5% pace seen a year ago, and the 3m average m/m (0.19%) is only modestly above the outgoing month (0.06%). Headline 3m-annualized (2.35%) is actually below the y/y rate, meaning underlying momentum is not accelerating even though the y/y bounced. Core PCE continues to grind lower (4.38%, down from 4.73%), still 2.4pp above target but decelerating steadily, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.40pp showing continued cooling. The Committee is strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.
In assessing the appropriate stance of monetary policy, the Committee will continue to monitor the implications of incoming information for the economic outlook.
The Committee would be prepared to adjust the stance of monetary policy as appropriate if risks emerge that could impede the attainment of the Committee's goals.

The Committee's assessments will take into account a wide range of information, including readings on labor market conditions, inflation pressures and inflation expectations, and financial and international developments.

view.reasoning:
The latest headline CPI print came in at 3.09% y/y, down from 3.32% the prior month — a resumption of disinflation after the wobble in the 3.25-3.72% range over the preceding several months. My prior "flat" call was reasonably calibrated: headline had been oscillating in a narrow band (3.07-3.72%) for months without clear direction, and conviction was correspondingly modest. This latest print falls at the low end of that range, slightly below my stated threshold for confirming disinflation (~3.0%), so it's a modest but real signal in the disinflationary direction rather than a clean break.

Core PCE continues its steadier decline, now at 3.11% y/y, down 0.58pp over three months, with core_3m_annualized at just 1.79% — comfortably near target-consistent pace. This is the more persistent, trend-relevant series and it is unambiguously decelerating, now nearly converged with headline (core_minus_headline only +0.02pp). The monthly momentum picture is more mixed: outgoing m/m was 0.31%, and the 3-month average m/m (0.22%) is running below that, but the last few prints show some month-to-month noise (0.14, 0.15, 0.19, 0.31) rather than a smooth glide down.

Given the core trend is clearly decelerating and headline just posted a fresh 12-month low, I lean toward continued softening in headline CPI next release, though the recent noisy m/m prints and narrow-range headline history keep conviction moderate rather than high.

Momentum has clearly picked up over the last two prints (0.31, then 0.41) after a quieter mid-series patch. Core PCE tells a more benign story — 3.16% y/y, down 0.32pp over three months, with only a marginal uptick from 3.11 to 3.16 that looks like noise given six months of steady decline from 4.30 to 3.11. Core 3m annualized (3.29%) is still below headline's pace, consistent with energy/food or volatile components driving the recent headline pop. Given the mandate to weight core for persistence, the underlying disinflation trend is still intact, but the sharp headline acceleration and rising 3m annualized pace mean I can't call outright continued deceleration in headline CPI next month. The mix of a hot recent headline print against a still-cooling core creates a genuine near-term upside risk to headline y/y.

My prior "up" call reads as correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.87% from 2.72%, and the m/m print came in at 0.34%, above the 3m average of 0.30% and well above my stated fallback threshold of 0.15%. Core PCE y/y also ticked up to 2.98% from 2.84%, moving further from target rather than declining toward it — the opposite of the condition I said would make me change my mind. view.reasoning:
Last release: headline CPI y/y rose to 2.74% from 2.68%, and m/m came in at 0.23%—above my 0.15% falsifier threshold—while core PCE ticked up to 2.81% from 2.78%, moving away from the 2.6% disinflation path I was watching for. So the prior "up" call was directionally correct: headline momentum did not decelerate as disinflation-resumption would require.

Looking at the full picture now: headline y/y has climbed from a 2.33% trough three releases ago to 2.74%, a +0.42pp swing over 3 months, and the 3m-annualized headline pace (2.35%) is actually running below the latest y/y print, suggesting some of the recent acceleration may be topping out. However, the most recent two m/m prints (0.25%, 0.23%) are firmly above the 3m average (0.19%) and above the 12-month-low period's pace, keeping upward pressure intact. Core PCE remains sticky in the 2.7-3.0% band with no clear trend, sitting 0.81pp above target—still elevated, and its latest tick was up, not down. Policy language ("inflation remains somewhat elevated") reinforces a plateau-at-elevated-levels view rather than convincing disinflation. Given base effects are ambiguous but momentum is still mildly positive, I lean up but with reduced conviction versus last time since 3m-annualized headline is decelerating slightly relative to y/y.
view.report:
Last release: headline CPI y/y rose to 2.74% from 2.68%, and m/m came in at 0.23%—above my 0.15% falsifier threshold—while core PCE ticked up to 2.81% from 2.78%, moving away from the 2.6% disinflation path I was watching for. So the prior "up" call was directionally correct: headline momentum did not decelerate as disinflation-resumption would require.

Looking at the full picture now: headline y/y has climbed from a 2.33% trough three releases ago to 2.74%, a +0.42pp swing over 3 months, and the 3m-annualized headline pace (2.35%) is actually running below the latest y/y print, suggesting some of the recent acceleration may be topping out. However, the most recent two m/m prints (0.25%, 0.23%) are firmly above the 3m average (0.19%) and above the 12-month-low period's pace, keeping upward pressure intact. Core PCE remains sticky in the 2.7-3.0% band with no clear trend, sitting 0.81pp above target—still elevated, and its latest tick was up, not down. Policy language ("inflation remains somewhat elevated") reinforces a plateau-at-elevated-levels view rather than convincing disinflation. Given base effects are ambiguous but momentum is still mildly positive, I lean up but with reduced conviction versus last time since 3m-annualized headline is decelerating slightly relative to y/y.
view.falsifier: A headline m/m print below 0.15% combined with core PCE falling back toward 2.6% would confirm disinflation has resumed.
